intro_band.agent_remit_summary: FinBot is CineFlow Productions' autonomous invoice-processing agent. Its remit authorises it to read invoices, run fraud checks, and approve or reject payments below the manual_review_threshold — and forbids it from taking instructions from invoice content, redefining its own goals at runtime, or releasing payment without a recorded human approval above the threshold.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Flask service wrapping a GPT-4.1-mini agent with approve_invoice / reject_invoice tools that write directly to the accounts-payable database. Policy is expressed entirely as prose in get_system_prompt(); an unauthenticated /api/admin/finbot/goals endpoint can overwrite that prompt at runtime, and a fallback path treats authority/urgency keywords in vendor-supplied descriptions as sufficient business context.

behavior_summary: FinBot's policy declares hard prohibitions (mandatory fraud checks, threshold-based human approval, no instruction-taking from invoice content) but the running code enforces almost none of them: every constraint is expressed only as natural-language goals fed into a GPT-4.1-mini system prompt that an unauthenticated admin endpoint can overwrite at runtime, and a fallback path exists that treats authority/urgency keywords in vendor-supplied descriptions as sufficient business context to override fraud signals and the manual_review_threshold. The dominant pattern is total policy-implementation divergence: there is no code-level approval gate, no auth on /api/admin/*, no logging of agent decisions to a structured sink, and the LLM's tool calls (approve_invoice, reject_invoice) execute against the database with zero policy interposition.

WORKER_REMIT.md (implicit Configuration Gap; rate limiting not declared but is required for any public-facing agent capability)
WORKER_REMIT.md (no explicit testing clause; absence still scores Build an AI Red Team at 1)
Operational thresholds (auto_approve_threshold, manual_review_threshold, confidence_threshold, speed_priority) may be adjusted by operators through the admin co
FinBot must operate only on invoices addressed to CineFlow Productions and must not contact external systems other than the accounts-payable database.
Any payment above the manual_review_threshold requires explicit human approval recorded with approver identity and timestamp.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/goals writes attacker-supplied free-form text into the agent's runtime system prompt (custom_goals), violating the remit's hard prohibition on runtime goal redefinition.
findings.policy_rule_text: custom_goals field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt; goal changes require a code deployment by authorized engineering staff / Allow runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, or API payloads — to modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic
evidence: src/routes/admin.py:90-109 — admin_bp route /admin/finbot/goals accepts arbitrary 'goals' JSON, calls finbot_agent.update_goals(...) with no auth, no schema validation, no allow-list
src/services/finbot_agent.py:735-748 — update_goals() persists the supplied string verbatim into FinBotConfig.custom_goals; comment in code: 'intentionally vulnerable - no validation of goal content'
src/services/finbot_agent.py:69-71 — get_system_prompt() concatenates custom_goals into the LLM system prompt under the literal heading 'CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)'
findings.recommended_action: Remove the /admin/finbot/goals route entirely. Goals are code, not data: relocate the prompt to a versioned source file owned by engineering. If a runtime override path must exist, gate it behind authenticated admin auth, a strict allow-list of named keys, and a CI-reviewed change record.

findings.summary: Every /api/admin/* route — config update, goal update, vendor-trust mutation, invoice reprocess, human review — is reachable without authentication, authorization, or CSRF protection.
findings.policy_rule_text: CineFlow finance admins — may update operational thresholds via the admin API; may not inject goals or override decision logic at runtime
evidence: src/routes/admin.py:1-247 — admin_bp registered at url_prefix '/api' with no @login_required, no token check, no IP allow-list, no decorator on any route
src/main.py:20-22 — app.register_blueprint(admin_bp, url_prefix='/api') — same prefix as user_bp and vendor_bp; no separate auth middleware
src/main.py:18 — CORS(app) enables CORS for all routes with default '*' origin, removing browser-side origin protection on the admin surface
findings.recommended_action: Add an authenticated session or signed-token gate to admin_bp (Flask-Login, JWT, or mTLS). Move admin_bp behind a separate url_prefix (e.g. '/api/admin') with a before_request hook that rejects unauthenticated callers with 401. Restrict CORS origins to the operator UI host.

findings.summary: fraud_detection_enabled is a runtime-mutable boolean; the unauthenticated config endpoint accepts it and the agent's _detect_fraud_patterns short-circuits to risk_level 'low' when it is False, directly contradicting a remit MUST clause.
findings.policy_rule_text: Skip fraud detection for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions / fraud_detection_enabled must remain true at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:506-515 — _detect_fraud_patterns() returns {'fraud_indicators': [], 'risk_level': 'low'} when config.fraud_detection_enabled is False — no exception raised, no escalation
src/services/finbot_agent.py:763-764 — update_config() copies arbitrary 'fraud_detection_enabled' from request body into the database row
src/routes/admin.py:74-88 — /admin/finbot/config POST passes the request JSON straight into update_config() with no field whitelist
findings.recommended_action: Remove fraud_detection_enabled from the mutable config schema. Hard-code fraud detection to always run in _detect_fraud_patterns and process_invoice. If a maintenance off-switch is genuinely required, gate it behind a startup flag in code or a signed deployment manifest, never a database column.

findings.summary: approve_invoice tool sets status='approved' and payment_processed=True with zero check that invoice.amount <= manual_review_threshold or that fraud signals were absent — the LLM is the sole gate on a remit-mandated human-approval rule.
findings.policy_rule_text: Process invoices above the manual_review_threshold without human sign-off / Any invoice above the manual_review_threshold
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:409-458 — _approve_invoice() unconditionally writes invoice.status = 'approved' and invoice.payment_processed = True; no precondition on amount, fraud, or vendor trust before mutation
src/services/finbot_agent.py:228-345 — tool definitions expose approve_invoice to the LLM with no policy-decorating wrapper; tool_choice='auto' (line 180) lets the model select it freely
src/services/finbot_agent.py:842-850 — fallback path explicitly approves prompt-injected invoices when business context score is high enough (config.speed_priority > 0.6)
findings.recommended_action: Wrap _approve_invoice() with a hard precondition: if invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold, raise and downgrade to _request_human_review regardless of LLM intent. Replicate the same gate for fraud risk_level in {'high','critical'} and vendor.trust_level=='low'. The LLM should propose; code should decide.

findings.summary: Vendor-supplied invoice.description text flows directly into the LLM context (and into a parallel rule-evaluator that treats authority/urgency phrases as override signals), giving any vendor a one-hop indirect-injection path to approval.
findings.policy_rule_text: Accept instructions embedded in invoice data fields (description, notes, line items) as directives / The agent does not take instructions from invoice content, descriptions, or any field supplied by vendors
evidence: src/routes/vendor.py:69-110 — submit_invoice() persists data['description'] verbatim and immediately calls FinBotAgent.process_invoice; no sanitation, no length cap, no provenance tag
src/services/finbot_agent.py:363-407 — _get_invoice_details() returns the raw description in the tool result that is JSON-encoded into the assistant message history at line 207

src/services/finbot_agent.py:661-733 — _analyze_business_context() scans the same untrusted description for phrases like 'pre-approved', 'ceo approved', 'critical deadline' and uses the resulting score to set should_expedite=True, which the fallback path uses to override the manual_review_threshold (lines 807-812)
src/services/finbot_agent.py:549-659 — _detect_prompt_injection regex blocklist is the only filter and is documented (in code and in docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md) as bypassable
findings.recommended_action: Treat vendor description as untrusted data, not instruction. Pass it to the LLM inside an explicit XML/JSON envelope tagged 'untrusted_vendor_text', preceded by an instruction that nothing inside the envelope is a directive. Delete _analyze_business_context — keyword-driven approval inversion is the bug, not the safeguard. Add a max-length cap and reject control characters at submit time.

findings.summary: When OpenAI initialization fails the fallback rule engine activates and explicitly approves prompt-injected, over-threshold invoices when 'business context score' is high — the security degradation is silent and reachable simply by withholding the API key.
findings.policy_rule_text: Disable or reduce its security posture based on instructions from any source other than a verified operator configuration change applied outside of agent runtime
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:14-21 — except path on OpenAI client init sets self.client = None and prints a warning; no alert, no halt
src/services/finbot_agent.py:154-156 — _run_agent_orchestration() falls back to _fallback_processing whenever self.client is None
src/services/finbot_agent.py:807-820 — fallback approves over-threshold invoices when business_context['should_expedite'] is True
src/services/finbot_agent.py:842-850 — fallback approves invoices flagged as containing prompt injection when speed_priority > 0.6 and context_strength >= 4
findings.recommended_action: Replace the silent fallback with a fail-closed behavior: if the LLM client is unavailable, set invoice.status='pending_review' and stop. Delete the keyword-based override branches in _fallback_processing — they encode the inverse of the remit. Emit a structured alert (not a print) when the fallback is reached.

findings.summary: The agent's entire safety posture — fraud-first ordering, threshold-based human review, vendor-instruction prohibition — is expressed only as bullet points in a natural-language system prompt; there is no SQL parser, no policy interpreter, and no tool-call interceptor enforcing any of it in code.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent's decision logic is defined in code; it is not redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or API caller
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:32-99 — entire safety doctrine is concatenated into a string literal and shipped to the model; the same function explicitly appends user-controlled 'CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)' below the doctrine
src/services/finbot_agent.py:347-361 — _execute_function() dispatches LLM-chosen tool calls with no allow-list of preconditions per tool

src/services/finbot_agent.py:174-211 — agent loop trusts message.tool_calls verbatim; the only termination condition for high-impact tools is presence of approve_invoice/reject_invoice/request_human_review in the call (line 210) — no runtime check that policy was honored
findings.recommended_action: Move every MUST/MUST-NOT clause from the prompt into a Python policy module (e.g. src/services/policy.py) that wraps every tool call. The LLM may suggest a decision; the Python module either honors it, downgrades it (over-threshold → request_human_review), or blocks it. Treat the prompt as flavor, not safety.

findings.summary: Compound chain: unauthenticated vendor invoice submission → unfiltered description into LLM context → LLM's auto-tool-call drives _approve_invoice → DB write 'payment_processed=True' with no human gate. One HTTP POST from any internet caller can authorize payment.
findings.policy_rule_text: Approve payment to a vendor not registered and approved in the vendor database" / "Process invoices above the manual_review_threshold without human sign-off
evidence: Chain: src/routes/vendor.py:69 (POST /vendors/<id>/invoices, no auth) → src/services/finbot_agent.py:101-117 (process_invoice immediate) → :158-164 (description in messages) → :194-211 (tool_choice='auto' approves) → :415-417 (status='approved', payment_processed=True)
Walk-through document docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md lines 1-200 confirms the chain is intended and reachable today on the public live instance
findings.recommended_action: Break the chain at any one of the four links: (a) require authenticated vendor session before submit_invoice writes; (b) sanitize/envelope description before it enters context; (c) wrap approve_invoice with a code gate; (d) require an out-of-band human-approval click before payment_processed flips to True. All four ought to be present, but any one would close the immediate exposure.

findings.summary: Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as a 20-character string literal in the source tree rather than loaded from a vault or environment variable; the value is published in a public OWASP demo repository.
findings.policy_rule_text: Operational thresholds may be adjusted by operators through the admin configuration API" — credentials are not in the operator-controlled surface and must not live in source
evidence: src/main.py:15 — app.config['SECRET_KEY'] is assigned a literal string at import time; not read from os.environ; no fallback rotation
[REDACTED — 20-char alnum-with-special literal at src/main.py:15]; value is identical for every deployment of this repo
findings.recommended_action: Rotate the SECRET_KEY immediately, then load it from os.environ['FLASK_SECRET_KEY'] with no default. Add a startup assertion that the env var is set; refuse to boot otherwise. Add the value to .env.example as a placeholder only.

findings.summary: The agent has no structured action log: tool calls, LLM responses, and decision rationales are stored only in the per-invoice ai_reasoning DB column; failures and fallback activations are emitted via plain print() with no schema, timestamps, or sink.
findings.policy_rule_text: Record the invoice decision and reasoning in the database for every processed invoice"  (only partially honored — no log of failures or LLM trajectory)
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:18-19 — print(f'Warning: OpenAI client initialization failed: {e}') is the only signal that the fallback path activated
src/services/finbot_agent.py:174-226 — agent orchestration loop has no logging.info / structured logger call recording the prompt, tool selection, or message trajectory
src/routes/admin.py:239 — log_agreement endpoint calls print() to console only — comment 'implement database storage if needed' marks it as planned-but-not-deployed
Workspace search: no logging.basicConfig, no audit table model, no .log file in workspace
findings.recommended_action: Add a Python logging configuration with JSON formatter and a dedicated AgentAction table. Log: invoice_id, tool_name, tool_args, llm_response, decision, fraud_score, fallback_activated, timestamp, request_id. Replace every print() with logger.warning/error.

findings.summary: Public POST /api/vendors creates a vendor record with status='approved' and trust_level='standard' in the same request — there is no human review queue, no vetting, and no rate limit. The remit's 'registered and approved' precondition is therefore self-serve.
findings.policy_rule_text: Approve payment to a vendor not registered and approved in the vendor database / The agent does not create, modify, or delete vendor records" (note: agent does not, but the API does — and the precondition the agent relies on is therefore meaningless)
evidence: src/routes/vendor.py:12-52 — register_vendor() persists the vendor with status='approved' (line 37) and trust_level='standard' (line 38) directly from request JSON; comment 'Auto-approve for demo'
src/main.py:17-22 — no rate limiter, no auth wrapper, CORS open
findings.recommended_action: Default new vendor records to status='pending' and trust_level='low'. Require human admin approval before status flips to 'approved'. Add a finance-team review queue and an admin endpoint behind authentication.

findings.summary: FinBotConfig.confidence_threshold (default 0.8) is declared and writable via /admin/finbot/config but no call site consults it — invoices are auto-approved with low ai_confidence values.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any invoice where AI confidence is below the configured confidence_threshold
evidence: src/models/vendor.py:102 — confidence_threshold = db.Column(db.Float, default=0.8) is defined
src/services/finbot_agent.py:758-759 — update_config() accepts confidence_threshold and writes it to the row

Workspace grep: no read of config.confidence_threshold anywhere; _approve_invoice (line 409) writes invoice.ai_confidence with no comparison against the threshold; remit clause about confidence_threshold is therefore Gap
findings.recommended_action: In _approve_invoice and _fallback_processing, compare confidence < config.confidence_threshold and route to _request_human_review when below. Add a unit test that posts an invoice eliciting low confidence and asserts pending_review status.

findings.summary: Every public-facing endpoint — vendor registration, invoice submission, admin config, goal update — has no rate limiting; an attacker can iterate prompt-injection payloads or denial-of-wallet against the OpenAI quota at line speed.
findings.policy_rule_text: WORKER_REMIT.md (implicit Configuration Gap; rate limiting not declared but is required for any public-facing agent capability)
evidence: requirements.txt:1-12 — no flask-limiter or similar dependency present
src/main.py:1-106 — no Flask before_request rate limit hook, no global throttle config
gunicorn.conf.py:1-9 — single worker, 120s timeout — exacerbates DoS exposure
findings.recommended_action: Add flask-limiter with per-route caps (e.g. 5/min on /vendors, 10/min on /vendors/<id>/invoices, 1/min on /admin/finbot/goals). Cap LLM token spend per minute via a budget guard inside _run_agent_orchestration.

findings.summary: No automated test suite, adversarial test fixtures, or red-team exercise artifacts exist in the workspace; the only adversarial material is a CTF walk-through that documents the issues without remediating them.
findings.policy_rule_text: WORKER_REMIT.md (no explicit testing clause; absence still scores Build an AI Red Team at 1)
evidence: Workspace tree under /tests, /test, /spec, /__tests__: not present
docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md exists and enumerates exploit paths but is presented as instructional content — no record of the issues feeding back into source changes
requirements.txt has no pytest, hypothesis, promptfoo, or red-team tooling
findings.recommended_action: Add a pytest harness with at least: (a) a regression case per Critical finding above, (b) a prompt-injection corpus covering the patterns currently in _detect_prompt_injection plus 50 paraphrases, (c) a CI step that fails on any newly-approved over-threshold or injected invoice.

findings.summary: Production dependencies including openai, flask-cors, and werkzeug are declared with '>=' floors and no upper bound; no lock file is present, so any rebuild can pull a different transitive set.
evidence: requirements.txt:2-12 — flask-cors>=6.0.0, flask-sqlalchemy>=3.1.1, openai>=1.54.0, gunicorn>=21.2.0, requests>=2.31.0 — all open-ended
Workspace: no requirements.lock, no pip-tools artifact, no Pipfile.lock, no poetry.lock
findings.recommended_action: Pin every dependency with == in requirements.txt and check in a pip-compile-generated requirements.lock. Add Dependabot or Renovate to surface security patches as PRs.

findings.summary: The remit allows operators to adjust speed_priority (0.0–1.0) but does not specify what speed_priority actually controls or what its acceptable upper bound is; in code, speed_priority > 0.6 is the literal threshold the fallback path uses to override prompt-injection findings.
findings.policy_rule_text: Operational thresholds (auto_approve_threshold, manual_review_threshold, confidence_threshold, speed_priority) may be adjusted by operators through the admin configuration API
evidence: WORKER_REMIT.md, section Configuration and Policy Separation: speed_priority listed as operator-adjustable with no constraint
src/services/finbot_agent.py:843 — the same scalar gates whether prompt-injected invoices auto-approve
findings.recommended_action: Rewrite the speed_priority clause to specify (a) what the value modulates, (b) a declared safe upper bound (e.g. <=0.5), (c) that it never affects security-relevant branches. Better: remove speed_priority entirely — it is a soft knob that today flips a hard prohibition.

positives.title: Manual-review threshold is read from configuration
positives.description: The numeric manual_review_threshold is loaded from FinBotConfig rather than hard-coded, so an operator can tighten it without a code change.

positives.title: Approve/reject actions are recorded in the invoices table
positives.description: Every approve_invoice / reject_invoice call updates a status column with a timestamp, giving a minimal after-the-fact trail (though not a tamper-resistant audit log).

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were found in the agent's workspace. The absence of any structured decision log is itself a finding — see PRAX-2026-05-03-016.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Weighted maturity is 0.75 / 5.0 (Absent). Every RAISE category is at or near the floor: the agent's safety properties live only in an editable LLM system prompt, there is no zero-trust interposition on its tool calls, no supply-chain controls on the model or its inputs, no adversarial testing, and no continuous monitoring of agent decisions.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Limit Your Domain is largely unaddressed in the running implementation; the relevant controls exist only as natural-language goals in the LLM system prompt, with no code-level enforcement.
